version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 5/5] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M
[PATCH v3 4/4] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch limits the iterations of the `shrink_delalloc` function in Btrfs to prevent it from blocking indefinitely, especially when dealing with large amounts of writeback data. The function is modified to process delalloc work in units of 128MiB, rather than trying to reclaim large chunks at once. This change aims to strike a balance between making progress on space reclaim and avoiding arbitrarily long syscall latencies and task hangs.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 4/5] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64
[PATCH v3 3/4] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the type of the 'outstanding_extents' field in the Btrfs inode structure from a u32 to a u64. This change is motivated by the fact that the maximum file size is LLONG_MAX, which would cause the max extent size computation to exceed the range of a u32 even when considering compressed cases. The patch also updates the 'mod_outstanding_extents' function to handle 64-bit arithmetic and adds assertions to ensure that the inputs are within valid ranges.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

version_history.subject: [PATCH 3/5] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation
[PATCH v2 3/5] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation
[PATCH v3 2/4] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue in the Btrfs file system where delayed reference reservations were under-estimated due to compression. When compression is enabled, the maximum extent size is reduced from 128MiB to 128KiB, leading to a significant discrepancy between the estimated and actual number of extents required for delalloc writes. The patch introduces a new function `btrfs_inode_max_extents` that takes into account the compressed extent size when calculating the number of outstanding extents. This change ensures accurate reservation of delayed reference space, even with compression enabled. Additionally, the patch simplifies the code by removing an unnecessary shim function and reducing the complexity of the calculation.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/5] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc
[PATCH v2 1/5] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc
[PATCH v3 1/4] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a problem in the Btrfs file system where space is not reserved for delayed references (delayed_refs) during delalloc operations, leading to incorrect assessments of available metadata space and poor decision making in reclaim logic. The patch adds a reservation for delayed_refs to the per-inode block_rsv, ensuring that enough space is allocated for these references. This fix aims to prevent issues such as erroneous preemptive reclaims and blocking start_transaction() calls due to insufficient reserved space.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that inserting an extent item in the extent tree requires COWing an extent tree leaf, creating a delayed tree ref for deletion, and requested additional consideration of this issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
Running the delayed data ref to insert the extent item in the extent
tree requires COWing an extent tree leaf, which creates a delayed tree
ref for deletion too.
So, 2.

Otherwise it looks good, thanks.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author Boris Burkov was addressing concerns about accurately reserving space for delayed refs in delalloc, specifically whether to count the number of cows caused by each delayed ref. He acknowledged that he might have been confused and proposed shifting the goal from counting add_delayed_ref calls to predicting metadata reservation due to delayed refs, agreeing with the reviewer's approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think I might be getting confused now :)

I'll try to work it out "out loud" and hopefully I can reach a good
conclusion.

I have been trying to reserve for the calls to add_delayed_ref in the
transaction handle context. And tracing each cow in that context yields
2 delayed refs each while tracing the data extent insertion only creates
one. The reason I care particularly about this context is the
trans_handle delayed_refs rsv accounting in
btrfs_update_delayed_refs_rsv(), as described in the patch. Ultimately,
all the reservations get sunk into the global reserve from
trans->delayed_rsv at btrfs_end_transaction(). Does this all sound right
to you so far?

Cows that we clearly do from finish_one_ordered() are the ones to:
raid stripe tree, subvol tree, and csum tree. Notably, the free space
tree *does not* happen in this context, it happens when we run the
delayed ref, so I think I was not consistent to include that. In that
context we cow the extent and free space tree for the data extent.

But the point of reserving for a delayed ref is to account for the cows
that it will ultimately cause and reserve ahead for them. So I think it
does make sense to reserve for the fst and extent tree. On the other
hand, I think we actively *don't* want to account for the potential
infinite regress where each metadata delayed_ref run "unluckily" results
in cow-ing the extent tree again and again since it doesn't actually
happen in practice (especially with Leo's writeback inhibition and
hopefully eb overwriting soon). That series diverges so it would require
an infinite reservation anyway.

In conclusion, I think I am agreeing and proposing that I shift the goal
from "count add_delayed_ref calls under the trans handle" to "predict
metadata reservation due to delayed refs" and in that world, it should
be 2 for the data extent in the extent tree.

The other principled option is to count 1 for the data delayed ref now
and not account for the fst or the extent tree cows that happen at
delayed_refs time, but I think that makes less sense.

Sorry if that was a lot of text to just agree with you, but I want to be
careful since it's easy to go astray and "get away with it" cause of the
reservations being conservative and getting sloshed into the global
reserves.

One final note / question:
the "normal" start_transaction() reservation which computes the space
for the cow itself and then the delayed_refs cows does *not* seem to
count 2 per cow for delayed refs in btrfs_calc_delayed_ref_bytes().
But in theory the same reasoning applies that we will touch two metadata
items for each cow.  I assume this is OK in practice because the depth
is generally not 8 anyway, but do you think that is something I should
clean up as well?

Thanks again,
Boris

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/5] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback
[PATCH v2 0/5] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback
[PATCH v3 0/4] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve stalls under sudden writeback in Btrfs file systems, particularly on large memory systems with high dirty ratios. The problem arises when a system accumulates hundreds of gigabytes of dirty pages before triggering writeback, leading to a tailspin of metadata space management and eventual system stall. To address this issue, the patches introduce improved reservations for delayed refs in delalloc work and basic smoothing of flush_space() heuristics. This is achieved through targeted changes to Btrfs inode accounting, types counting, and shrink_delalloc iteration capping.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] mm: kick writeback flusher instead of inline flush for IOCB_DONTCACHE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a performance issue in the IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path by moving writeback submission off the writer's hot path. Instead of submitting writeback inline, it uses a background flusher thread to handle writeback asynchronously and coalescence I/O without explicit skip-if-busy or dirty pressure checks. The patch introduces a new function `filemap_dontcache_kick_writeback` that wakes up the BDI flusher thread with a specific reason for tracing visibility.

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Ritesh pointed out that the new API `filemap_dontcache_kick_writeback` gives a misleading sense of kicking writeback for only an inode's address space, when in fact it starts writeback for all dirty pages on the respective BDI. He suggested exporting the symbol for `wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi()` and using it instead, or modifying the API to make its purpose clearer.
review_comments.raw_body:
This api gives a wrong sense that we are kicking writeback to write
dirty pages which belongs to only this inode's address space mapping.
But instead we are starting wb for everything on the respective bdi.

So instead why not just export symbol for wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi()
and use it instead?

If not, then IMO at least making it... 
   filemap_kick_writeback_all(mapping, enum wb_reason)

... might be better.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the current implementation of filemap_dontcache_kick_writeback causes a WB_SYNC_NONE writeback every time a DONTCACHE write finishes, which is not desired, and suggested introducing a new WB_start_dontcache bit to handle this case.
review_comments.raw_body:
wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi ends up calling wb_start_writeback eventually,
which sets WB_start_all, pushes the reason to start_all_reason and then
does the actual wakeup.

The flusher thread then through wb_check_start_all does a WB_SYNC_NONE
writeback based on get_nr_dirty_pages.  Which seems wrong - we don't
want to do a huge writeback evertime the some DONTCACHE write finished.

So I think you'll want a new WB_start_dontcache bit, a new
get_nr_dontcache_pages() helper on a new node counter, etc.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Christoph Hellwig suggested that the new WB_REASON_DONTCACHE reason for tracing visibility should not be exposed to userspace, and also requested that the function wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi() be marked as static in fs-writeback.c.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'd rather not expose that to wide with the extra reason arguments
that's not otherwise exposed outside the core writeback code.

Btw, wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi should really be marked static in
fs-writeback.c as well.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/3] testing: add nfsd-io-bench NFS server benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new benchmark suite, nfsd-io-bench, to test the performance of NFSD I/O modes using fio and libnfs against an NFS server on localhost. The suite includes various workloads such as sequential and random read/write, multi-writer, noisy-neighbor, and latency-sensitive reader tests. It also includes scripts for setting up the NFS export, running the benchmarks, and parsing the results from fio's JSON output. This patch aims to provide a comprehensive testing framework for NFSD I/O performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] testing: add dontcache-bench local filesystem benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new benchmark suite called 'dontcache-bench' to test the IOCB_DONTCACHE flag on local filesystems using fio's io_uring engine. The suite includes various job files and scripts to run and parse the results of these tests, mirroring the existing nfsd-io-bench test matrix but with different I/O modes. This patch aims to evaluate the performance impact of disabling caching for specific I/O operations on local filesystems.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch set improves write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag by kicking the flusher thread for the superblock instead of initiating writeback directly. This change is based on benchmarking results that show a clear improvement over the status quo, particularly in cases with heavy write activity. The patch also includes a comparison of different IO modes for nfsd and highlights the poor performance of RWF_DONTCACHE with write-heavy workloads. The approach taken involves modifying the generic_write_sync function to kick the flusher thread, which is expected to better isolate the DONTCACHE writeback behavior and improve overall performance.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing concerns about the performance of the per-inode writeback approach, specifically that it regresses on multi-writer and random write workloads due to lost I/O batching. The author acknowledges this regression and suggests sticking with v2, which has a different approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
Actually, that version regressed performance in a couple of cases. I think v2 is probably the best approach, on balance. Maybe we can get this into -next so that it can make v7.2?

Here's the comparison between this version and a writeback_single_inode() flush version:

------------------8<-----------------------

● Comparing dontcache numbers against the previous whole-BDI flusher kernel (from /tmp/dontcache-local-4way-flusher.md):                                                                          

  Per-Inode vs Whole-BDI Flusher — DONTCACHE on Local XFS                                                                                                                                         

  Single-Client Writes

  ┌──────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬─────────────┐                                                                                                                                      
  │    Benchmark     │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │   Change    │                                                                                                                                      
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤      
  │ Seq write MB/s   │ 1450      │ 1438      │ -1% (noise) │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Seq write p99.9  │ 23.5 ms   │ 23.5 ms   │ identical   │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤                                                                                                                                      
  │ Rand write MB/s  │ 363       │ 286       │ -21%        │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤                                                                                                                                      
  │ Rand write p99.9 │ 1.8 ms    │ 16.7 ms   │ regression  │
  └──────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴─────────────┘

  Seq write is identical. Rand write regressed — the whole-BDI flusher batched all dirty pages in one pass with writeback_sb_inodes() under a single blk_plug, while per-inode write_inode_now() loses that batching.

  Single-Client Reads

  ┌────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬────────┐
  │   Benchmark    │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │ Change │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────┤
  │ Seq read MB/s  │ 2950      │ 2350      │ -20%   │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────┤
  │ Rand read MB/s │ 651       │ 519       │ -20%   │
  └────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴────────┘

  Reads shouldn't be affected by writeback path changes. Buffered reads also dropped (2888 → 2331), suggesting different system conditions between runs rather than a per-inode regression.

  Multi-Writer (Scenario A)

  ┌────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬────────────┐
  │     Metric     │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │   Change   │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────────┤
  │ Aggregate MB/s │ 1478      │ 999       │ -32%       │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────────┤
  │ p99.9          │ 46 ms     │ 77 ms     │ -67% worse │
  └────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴────────────┘

  This is the biggest regression. With whole-BDI, the flusher did one batched pass through all dirty inodes via writeback_sb_inodes(). With per-inode, each of 4 writers queues a separate work item processed serially by write_inode_now() — losing the batch I/O merging benefit.

  Scenario C & D (Noisy Neighbor)

  ┌─────────────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬─────────────┐
  │         Metric          │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │   Change    │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario C writer       │ 1468      │ 1386      │ -6%         │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario C readers      │ 18.7 MB/s │ 18.7 MB/s │ identical   │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario D writer       │ 1472      │ 1467      │ identical   │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario D readers      │ 496 MB/s  │ 507 MB/s  │ +2%         │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario D reader p99.9 │ 440 us    │ 358 us    │ +19% better │
  └─────────────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴─────────────┘

  Mixed-mode (Scenario D) is the intended production case and it's essentially identical or slightly better — per-inode writeback creates less device contention for buffered readers.

  Summary

  The per-inode approach is neutral-to-slightly-better for the production scenario (Scenario D), but regresses on multi-writer and random write workloads. The core issue is loss of I/O batching
 — writeback_sb_inodes() processes all dirty inodes in one blk_plug'd pass, while per-inode write_inode_now() calls are processed one at a time. The read regressions likely reflect different
  system conditions since buffered/direct reads also dropped ~20%.

-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 24/24] nfsd: add support to CB_NOTIFY for dir attribute changes

version_history.subject: [PATCH 24/24] nfsd: add support to CB_NOTIFY for dir attribute changes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 00/24] vfs/nfsd: add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations, allowing clients to register interest in a directory and receive notifications about changes within it without losing their lease. The patches introduce new functionality to the Linux kernel's NFS server (nfsd) to handle directory events such as creates, unlinks, and renames, and to send updated directory attributes in notifications. This is built on top of previous work on directory delegation and has been tested with pynfs. The series includes 24 patches that modify various parts of the kernel, including file locking, notification handling, and attribute encoding.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern that the server should not recall a directory delegation when a client requests it with a notification bitmask covering directory change events. The author agreed to add support for ignoring lease breaks on directory changes by modifying try_break_deleg() and __break_lease().

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)

> @@ -1597,6 +1597,38 @@ any_leases_conflict(struct inode *inode, struct file_lease *breaker)

> +#define IGNORE_MASK	(FL_IGN_DIR_CREATE | FL_IGN_DIR_DELETE | FL_IGN_DIR_RENAME)

> +/**
> + * inode_lease_ignore_mask - return union of all ignored inode events for this inode
> + * @inode: inode of which to get ignore mask

> + * Walk the list of leases, and return the result of all of

> +}

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Chuck Lever noted that after breaking visible leases, the restart: label in __break_lease() calls any_leases_conflict(), which does not filter ignored dir-delegation leases. This causes unnecessary work and potential spin loops when only ignored leases remain. He suggested skipping ignored leases in this case.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 01/24] filelock: add support for ignoring deleg breaks for dir change events
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support to ignore delegation breaks for directory change events in the Linux kernel's filelock module. The patch introduces a new flags parameter to try_break_deleg() and modifies __break_lease to ignore certain types of delegation break events, specifically those related to directory changes such as creation, deletion, or renaming. This allows NFS clients to be notified of directory changes after the fact instead of having the server recall the delegation immediately. The patch also includes modifications to several other files in the kernel's file system code to accommodate this new behavior.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that after breaking visible leases, the code calls any_leases_conflict() which doesn't filter out ignored dir-delegation leases, causing a loop when only ignored leases remain.
review_comments.raw_body:
After breaking visible leases, the restart: label calls any_leases_conflict()
which does not filter ignored dir-delegation leases. When only ignored leases
remain, any_leases_conflict returns true, but visible_leases_remaining also
returned true (triggering the wait). The code picks the first lease (possibly
ignored), computes break_time = 1 jiffy, blocks, then loops.                                                     

For example, suppose you have two directory delegations on a directory, one
with FL_IGN_DIR_DELETE and one without. After the non-ignored one is broken
and removed, the ignored one keeps any_leases_conflict returning true. The
loop spins at 1-jiffy intervals until the ignored delegation is released.  

Should the restart: block skip ignored leases?


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a mistake in the patch, agreed to fix it by replacing any_leases_conflict() call with visible_leases_remaining(), and promised to test it.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yep. The right fix is to switch the any_leases_conflict() call to
visible_leases_remaining(). I'll code that up and test it.

Thanks for the review!
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 02/24] filelock: add a tracepoint to start of break_lease()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new tracepoint to the filelock subsystem, specifically at the start of the break_lease() function. The goal is to provide more detailed information about lease breaks, including flags that indicate specific reasons for breaking the lease.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 03/24] filelock: add an inode_lease_ignore_mask helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new helper function, inode_lease_ignore_mask, which returns a mask of all directory change events currently ignored by any leases. The function is used to determine how to configure the fsnotify_mark mask for nfsd. The patch introduces a new routine that walks through the list of leases and returns the result of all their FL_IGN_DIR_* bits or'ed together.

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary: The patch looks good and the reviewer is happy to add their Reviewed-by tag. They suggest adding this tag in the future.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 04/24] nfsd: add protocol support for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds protocol support for CB_NOTIFY in the nfsd module. It updates the xdr files and removes duplicate definitions from various header files. The changes include adding new enum values, updating typedefs, and regenerating the xdr files. This is part of a larger series of patches that aim to improve the NFSv4.x protocol implementation in the Linux kernel.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 05/24] nfs_common: add new NOTIFY4_* flags proposed in RFC8881bis
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds new flags to the NFSv4 protocol, specifically NOTIFY4_* flags proposed in RFC8881bis. These flags are added to the GET_DIR_DELEGATION operation and include various notification types such as AUFLAG_VALID, AUFLAG_USER, and CHANGE_AUTH. The changes also update the XDR specification file and the generated code for the nfsd kernel module. This patch is part of a larger series that aims to support new features in NFSv4.1 bis document.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 06/24] nfsd: allow nfsd to get a dir lease with an ignore mask
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the NFS daemon to obtain a directory lease with an ignore mask, enabling it to filter out specific notification types such as file creation, deletion, and renaming. The ignore mask is determined by the requested notification types, allowing for more fine-grained control over which events trigger notifications. This change affects the nfsd component of the Linux kernel.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 07/24] vfs: add fsnotify_modify_mark_mask()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new function, fsnotify_modify_mark_mask(), which allows the caller to set and clear bits in an existing mark's mask. The function is used by nfsd to modify directory delegations. It also updates the associated connector if necessary.

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary: Jan Kara approved the patch and suggested adding a Reviewed-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue 07-04-26 09:21:20, Jeff Layton wrote:
> nfsd needs to be able to modify the mask on an existing mark when new
> directory delegations are set or unset. Add an exported function that

> 
> Suggested-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

Looks good. Feel free to add:

Reviewed-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>

								Honza


> ---

> +/**
> + * fsnotify_modify_mark_mask - set and/or clear flags in a mark's mask
> + * @mark: mark to be modified
> + * @set: bits to be set in mask
> + * @clear: bits to be cleared in mask
> + *
> + * Modify a fsnotify_mark mask as directed, and update its associated conn.
> + * The caller is expected to hold a reference to the mark.
> + */
> +void fsnotify_modify_mark_mask(struct fsnotify_mark *mark, u32 set, u32 clear)
> +{
> +	bool recalc = false;
> +	u32 mask;

> +	if (mark->mask != mask)

> +	spin_unlock(&mark->lock);
> +
> +	if (recalc)
> +		fsnotify_recalc_mask(mark->connector);
> +}
> +EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(fsnotify_modify_mark_mask);

series_items.subject: [PATCH 08/24] nfsd: update the fsnotify mark when setting or removing a dir delegation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series updates the NFSv4 server to correctly handle directory delegations and their associated fsnotify marks. The main change in this specific patch is the addition of a new helper function, nfsd_fsnotify_recalc_mask(), which updates the fsnotify mark when setting or removing a dir delegation.

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary: Jan Kara found the patch to be good and suggested adding a Reviewed-by tag.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Chuck Lever pointed out a potential issue with the ordering of grant path operations in nfsd_get_dir_deleg(), which could leave stale fsnotify marks. He suggested calling nfsd_fsnotify_recalc_mask() after kernel_setlease(F_UNLCK) in nfs4_unlock_deleg_lease().
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Apr 7, 2026, at 9:21 AM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> Add a new helper function that will update the mask on the nfsd_file's
> fsnotify_mark to be a union of all current directory delegations on an
> inode. Call that when directory delegations are added or removed.
>
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

> diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c
> index c8fb84c38637..9a4cff08c67d 100644
> --- a/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c
> +++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c

> @@ -1266,6 +1297,7 @@ static void nfs4_unlock_deleg_lease(struct 
> nfs4_delegation *dp)
>  	WARN_ON_ONCE(!fp->fi_delegees);
> 
>  	nfsd4_finalize_deleg_timestamps(dp, nf->nf_file);
> +	nfsd_fsnotify_recalc_mask(nf);
>  	kernel_setlease(nf->nf_file, F_UNLCK, NULL, (void **)&dp);
>  	put_deleg_file(fp);
>  }

The grant path in nfsd_get_dir_deleg() uses a different ordering
(setlease first, recalc_mask after).

Here, since the delegation being removed is still in flc_lease,
inode_lease_ignore_mask() includes its ignore flags. The mask is
computed as if the delegation is still present.

The result is that stale FS_CREATE/FS_DELETE/FS_RENAME bits remain
in the fsnotify mark. It might be harmless in practice since the
handler finds no leases and returns early, but it creates
unnecessary work.

Should nfs4_unlock_deleg_lease call nfsd_fsnotify_recalc_mask()
after kernel_setlease(F_UNLCK)?


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Jeff Layton acknowledged the issue and agreed to fix it.

> >

> > +	nfsd_fsnotify_recalc_mask(nf);
> >  	kernel_setlease(nf->nf_file, F_UNLCK, NULL, (void **)&dp);
> >  	put_deleg_file(fp);
> >  }
> 
> The grant path in nfsd_get_dir_deleg() uses a different ordering

> 
> Here, since the delegation being removed is still in flc_lease,
> inode_lease_ignore_mask() includes its ignore flags. The mask is

series_items.subject: [PATCH 09/24] nfsd: make nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation bool return
series_items.patch_summary: This patch changes the nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation to return a boolean value indicating whether processing should continue or not. The patch modifies several callback operations, including CB_NOTIFY, layout preparation, and recall preparation, to return true if successful and false otherwise. This change allows for better error handling in case of allocation failures during callback processing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 10/24] nfsd: add callback encoding and decoding linkages for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds routines for encoding and decoding CB_NOTIFY messages in the NFSv4 callback protocol. It introduces new functions to encode and decode the CB_NOTIFY message, which is used to notify a client of changes on the server. The encoding and decoding processes involve calling into code generated by xdrgen to perform the actual encoding and decoding. This patch also updates the nfsd/nfs4callback.c file to include the new encoding and decoding functions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 11/24] nfsd: use RCU to protect fi_deleg_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds RCU (Read-Copy Update) protection to the fi_deleg_file pointer in the nfsd kernel module. The patch annotates the pointer with __rcu and converts all accessors to use proper RCU primitives, such as rcu_assign_pointer() and rcu_dereference_protected(). This change is necessary because concurrent accesses from workqueue callbacks can dereference a NULL pointer if they race with delegation teardown. The patch prepares for a subsequent change that will safely acquire a reference from the CB_NOTIFY callback path without holding the fi_lock lock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 12/24] nfsd: add data structures for handling CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds data structures and callback operations for handling directory delegation CB_NOTIFY in the NFS server. It introduces two new structs: nfsd_notify_event, which carries fsnotify events, and nfsd4_cb_notify, which holds per-delegation state for notification handling. The patch also refactors alloc_init_deleg to a common base function with a pluggable sc_free callback and adds alloc_init_dir_deleg to allocate resources needed for CB_NOTIFY encoding. Additionally, it sets up skeleton operations for the notification path, which will be filled in later.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that events arriving during the retry window after NFS4ERR_DELAY are dropped, causing clients to miss notifications due to nfsd4_run_cb_notify() returning early when NFSD4_CALLBACK_RUNNING is set.
review_comments.raw_body:
So when a client responds with NFS4ERR_DELAY, the RPC framework retries
after 2s. On retry, prepare() is called again, but ncn_evt_cnt is
already 0 (drained in the first prepare). prepare returns false, which
destroys the callback.

Events arriving during the retry window are dropped because
nfsd4_run_cb_notify() returns early when NFSD4_CALLBACK_RUNNING is set.
After the callback is destroyed, future events can queue a new CB_NOTIFY,
but the window's events are lost.                                                                                        

The result is that the client misses notifications. Does this impact
behavioral correctness or spec compliance? Is there a way for that
client to detect the loss and recover?


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed Chuck Lever's concern about nfsd4_cb_prepare being called twice, explaining that rpc_restart_call_prepare retries the RPC through the RPC-level prepare without calling cb_ops->prepare again.

Author acknowledged a problem with the ->release handler queueing up events, agreed to fix it in a future version.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is actually not a problem. When ->done() returns 0,
rpc_restart_call_prepare retries the RPC through the RPC-level prepare
(nfsd4_cb_prepare at nfs4callback.c:1479), which just acquires a
session slot and calls rpc_call_start. It does not call cb_ops->prepare
again — the same encoded XDR is re-sent in that case.

---

There _is_ a problem, however. Events that arrive while the job is
running queue up, but won't get sent until the _next_ event arrives. We
need to make the ->release handler check for new events and requeue the
callback if there are any. I'll plan to fix that up.

Thanks for all the review! I'll plan to send another version soon (but
probably not until after next week's testing event).
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 13/24] nfsd: add notification handlers for dir events
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for directory change events in the NFS server (nfsd). It introduces a new fsnotify group for directories and modifies existing code to use this new group when handling file marks. The patch also includes changes to handle notifications for delegation callbacks and to allocate nfsd_notify_event objects. If any errors occur during these operations, affected delegations are recalled. This patch is part of a larger series of 24 patches.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the current implementation fails to send notifications for cross-directory renames due to incorrect flag handling, specifically failing to check FL_IGN_DIR_RENAME instead of FL_IGN_DIR_DELETE in should_notify_deleg().
review_comments.raw_body:
For a cross-directory rename, vfs_rename calls try_break_deleg(old_dir,
LEASE_BREAK_DIR_DELETE, ...). A delegation with FL_IGN_DIR_DELETE
(subscribed to NOTIFY4_REMOVE_ENTRY) suppresses the lease break, which
is correct.

But fsnotify delivers FS_RENAME on old_dir, not FS_DELETE. In
should_notify_deleg(), the check (mask & FS_RENAME) &&
!(fl->c.flc_flags & FL_IGN_DIR_RENAME) fails, because the delegation
has FL_IGN_DIR_DELETE but not FL_IGN_DIR_RENAME. No notification is
sent.

IIUC, a client subscribed to NOTIFY4_REMOVE_ENTRY for old_dir sees
neither a lease break nor a CB_NOTIFY when a child is renamed out of
the directory. Is that behavior correct?

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the patch needs to handle FS_RENAME_FROM and FS_RENAME_TO events in addition to FS_CREATE/DELETE, agreed to add a fix for this issue.
sentiment_signals: acknowledged a need for a fix
review_comments.raw_body:
Actually, it delivers FS_RENAME_FROM to the old directory and
FS_RENAME_TO to the new one in this case, AFAICT. Basically, we need to
watch for those in addition to FS_CREATE/DELETE and treat them the same
way. I'm looking at a fix now. I'll also see about adding a pynfs test
to do a cross directory rename with notifications. I don't think I have
that now.

Thanks!

series_items.subject: [PATCH 14/24] nfsd: add tracepoint to dir_event handler
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new tracepoint to the NFS server's dir_event handler, providing extra visibility into file system events. The change is implemented in two files: nfsd/nfs4state.c and fs/nfsd/trace.h.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 15/24] nfsd: apply the notify mask to the delegation when requested
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the NFSv4 server to apply the requested notification mask to the delegation when a client requests a directory delegation with notifications enabled. The patch ensures that if the client doesn't set the NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND flag, no notifications are offered, as the server won't provide necessary information for classic notifications. The patch also defines a constant for supported notify masks and updates the get_dir_delegation function to apply the requested notification mask.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 16/24] nfsd: add helper to marshal a fattr4 from completed args
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code that encodes attribute values in an NFSv4 file attribute (fattr4) from a completed operation. It breaks out the encoding of actual attr_vals into a separate function, nfsd4_encode_attr_vals(), which takes a struct xdr_stream and a u32 bitmap as arguments. This change simplifies the code and makes it easier to maintain.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 17/24] nfsd: allow nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change() to work with no export
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change function to work even when there is no export available, which can happen in certain callback scenarios such as CB_NOTIFY. This is safe because nfsd will not grant a delegation on the V4 root. The change involves modifying the condition under which an error is returned from this function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 18/24] nfsd: send basic file attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to send basic inode information in a CB_NOTIFY event, including attributes that can be acquired via GETATTR. The patch introduces new code to handle this functionality and includes error handling for cases where the client was granted a delegation when a file was created. The changes are made to the nfsd4xdr.c file in the Linux kernel.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 19/24] nfsd: allow encoding a filehandle into fattr4 without a svc_fh
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the encoding of a filehandle into fattr4 without requiring a svc_fh. It achieves this by adding a new 'fhandle' field to struct nfsd4_fattr_args and copying the filehandle into it from the svc_fh in CB_NOTIFY callbacks. This change enables the encoding of filehandles in scenarios where a svc_fh is not available, such as in CB_NOTIFY callbacks.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 20/24] nfsd: add a fi_connectable flag to struct nfs4_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag, fi_connectable, to the nfs4_file structure in the Linux NFS server. The flag indicates whether a filehandle is connectable or not, which is necessary for encoding filehandles in certain situations, such as CB_NOTIFY operations. The patch modifies the nfsd4_file_init function to set this flag based on the export flags of the underlying directory delegation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 21/24] nfsd: add the filehandle to returned attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new routine to compose a filehandle in the context of a CB_NOTIFY, where there is no export to consult. The routine uses only the parent filehandle and nfs4_file to fill out the fhandle field in the nfsd4_fattr_args. This allows for the return of a filehandle in the attributes of a CB_NOTIFY response.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 22/24] nfsd: properly track requested child attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of child attributes in NFSv4 delegation by tracking the union of requested and supported attributes, and only encoding the attributes in that union when sending add/remove/rename updates. This ensures that only relevant attributes are sent to the client, reducing unnecessary data transfer and improving performance. The patch also introduces new macros to define the bitmasks for child attributes, making the code more readable and maintainable.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 23/24] nfsd: track requested dir attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of directory attributes in NFSv4 by tracking the union of requested and supported dir attributes in the delegation, and only encoding the attributes in that union when sending add/remove/rename updates. This ensures that only valid and supported attributes are sent to the client, preventing potential errors or inconsistencies. The patch also introduces new data structures to store the directory attributes and notification masks, making it easier to manage and update these values. Overall, this patch enhances the reliability and robustness of NFSv4 by improving its handling of directory attributes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds support to CB_NOTIFY for dir attribute changes in the NFSv4 server. It allows clients that have requested notifications of directory attribute changes to receive these updates alongside other events such as add, remove, and rename operations. The server still recalls the delegation on a SETATTR operation, so these notifications are only sent when there is a change to child directories. The patch modifies two files: nfsd/nfs4state.c and fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c, adding new code to handle directory attribute changes and update the notify mask accordingly.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] dcache: warn when a dentry is freed with a non-empty ->d_lru

version_history.subject: [PATCH] dcache: warn when a dentry is freed with a non-empty ->d_lru

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a warning when a dentry is freed while its ->d_lru list is not empty, which can indicate an use-after-free (UAF) bug. The problem was observed in hosts with heavy process churn and resulted in panics during /proc entry invalidation. The patch aims to make it easier to spot the underlying issue by adding a WARN_ON_ONCE() condition when freeing a dentry with a non-empty ->d_lru list.

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the WARN_ON_ONCE() in dentry_free() should be a WARN_ON() instead, as it's not an occasional issue but rather a regular occurrence.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes, looks like a sensible assert. Feel free to add:

Reviewed-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>

								Honza

review_comments.author: Al Viro
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Al Viro suspects that the dentry pointer passed to shrink_dcache_parent() is garbage due to struct pid having already been freed, and suggests obtaining a crash dump for further investigation.
review_comments.raw_body:
That, or dentry pointer passed to shrink_dcache_parent() is a

Which kernels have you seen it on?

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the dentry->d_lru list is not always empty when a dentry is freed, but stated they are trying to obtain a coredump from one of the crashing machines.

Author acknowledged that the workload-dependent nature of the crash may be due to a fix in later kernels, but still sees value in adding a warning for dentry freeing.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm trying to get one. We had an issue that prevented the machines that
were crashing this way from getting a coredump. Hoping that'll be
resolved soon and we can get it.

---

v6.11 and v6.13 so far. The crash seems to be pretty workload-dependent
(a lot of processes rapidly starting and exiting). I'm not sure this
workload is running on later kernels yet so I don't know if this is
something already fixed.

Thanks,
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)

## dcache: `shrink_dcache_parent()` livelock leading to use-after-free

### Summary

A race between concurrent proc dentry invalidation (`proc_flush_pid` →
`d_invalidate` → `shrink_dcache_parent`) and the global dentry shrinker
(`drop_caches` / memory pressure → `prune_dcache_sb`) causes
`shrink_dcache_parent()` to loop indefinitely. This livelock is the
root cause of the use-after-free crash observed in production (see
P2260313060 for the original crash analysis).

### How the bug manifests

**In production** (narrow race window): The livelock occasionally
resolves through specific timing that allows a parent dentry to be
freed and its slab page reused. When a sibling's `__dentry_kill` then
tries `spin_lock(&parent->d_lock)` on the reused memory → page fault in
`queued_spin_lock_slowpath` (Oops).

**With `CONFIG_DCACHE_SHRINK_RACE_DEBUG`** (5ms delay in
`__dentry_kill`): The race is deterministic. `shrink_dcache_parent()`
livelocks on the first iteration and never completes.

### Root cause

In `select_collect()` (the `d_walk` callback used by
`shrink_dcache_parent`), two types of dentries are incorrectly counted
as "found":

1. **Dead dentries** (`d_lockref.count < 0`): Another CPU called
`lockref_mark_dead()` in `__dentry_kill()` but hasn't yet called
`dentry_unlist()` to remove the dentry from the parent's children list.
With the debug delay, the dentry stays dead-but-visible for 5ms.

2. **`DCACHE_SHRINK_LIST` dentries**: Already isolated by another
shrinker path (e.g., the global LRU shrinker from `drop_caches`) to its
own dispose list. These are being processed by that other path but
slowly (5ms per proc dentry with the debug delay).

When `select_collect` counts these as `found++`,
`shrink_dcache_parent()` sees `data.found > 0` and loops again. But
these dentries can never be collected onto `data.dispose` (dead ones
have count < 0, shrink-list ones already have `DCACHE_SHRINK_LIST`
set), so the loop never makes progress → **infinite loop**.

```
shrink_dcache_parent() loop:
  for (;;) {
      d_walk(parent, &data, select_collect);
      if (!list_empty(&data.dispose)) {
          shrink_dentry_list(&data.dispose);  // never reached
          continue;
      }
      if (!data.found)
          break;            // never reached because found > 0
      // ... loops forever
  }
```

### Reproducer

**Requirements:**
- `CONFIG_DCACHE_SHRINK_RACE_DEBUG=y` (injects 5ms `mdelay()` in
`__dentry_kill` for proc dentries)
- `CONFIG_KASAN=y` (optional, for UAF detection)
- `CONFIG_DEBUG_KERNEL=y`

**Debug patch** (apply to `fs/Kconfig` and `fs/dcache.c`):

```diff
--- a/fs/Kconfig
+++ b/fs/Kconfig
@@ -9,6 +9,15 @@ menu "File systems"
 config DCACHE_WORD_ACCESS
        bool
 
+config DCACHE_SHRINK_RACE_DEBUG
+	bool "Debug: inject delay in __dentry_kill to widen race
window"
+	depends on DEBUG_KERNEL
+	default n
+	help
+	  Inject a delay in __dentry_kill() between releasing d_lock
and
+	  re-acquiring it, to make the shrink_dentry_list race
reproducible
+	  in test environments. Only enable for testing.
+
 config VALIDATE_FS_PARSER

--- a/fs/dcache.c
+++ b/fs/dcache.c
@@ -32,6 +32,7 @@
 #include <linux/list_lru.h>
+#include <linux/delay.h>
 #include "internal.h"
 
@@ -630,6 +631,16 @@ static struct dentry *__dentry_kill(...)
 	cond_resched();
+#ifdef CONFIG_DCACHE_SHRINK_RACE_DEBUG
+	/*
+	 * Delay proc dentry kills to keep dead dentries in the tree
+	 * longer. With the bug (count < 0 counted as "found" in
+	 * select_collect), d_walk keeps re-finding dead dentries and
+	 * shrink_dcache_parent() loops forever.
+	 */
+	if (dentry->d_sb->s_magic == 0x9fa0 /* PROC_SUPER_MAGIC */)
+		mdelay(5);
+#endif
 	/* now that it's negative, ->d_parent is stable */
```

**Test program** (`test_dcache_race.sh`):

The reproducer creates multi-threaded processes, populates their
`/proc/<pid>/task/<tid>/...` dcache entries, then SIGKILLs them while
simultaneously running `drop_caches` in tight loops. This creates
concurrent `proc_flush_pid` (from dying threads) and `prune_dcache_sb`
(from `drop_caches`) paths competing on the same proc dentries.

```c
/* Key structure:
 * - Fork child with N threads (creates /proc/<pid>/task/<tid>/...
entries)
 * - Parent reads all /proc entries to populate dcache
 * - Background threads continuously do: echo 2 >
/proc/sys/vm/drop_caches
 * - SIGKILL child -> all threads exit -> concurrent proc_flush_pid
 * - drop_caches shrinker races with proc_flush_pid on same dentries
 */
```

Parameters used: 50 threads/process, 200 iterations, 4 shrinker
threads, 4 reader threads.

**vmtest.toml:**
```toml
[[target]]
name = "dcache-shrink-race"
kernel = "arch/x86/boot/bzImage"
kernel_args = "hung_task_panic=0 softlockup_panic=0
rcupdate.rcu_cpu_stall_suppress=1"
command = "/mnt/vmtest/test_dcache_race.sh"

  [target.vm]
  memory = "16G"   # KASAN needs extra memory
  num_cpus = 8
  timeout = 1200
```

### Reproduction results

| Kernel | Result |
|---|---|
| Unfixed + debug delay + KASAN | **FAIL**: livelock on iteration 1,
test timed out at 750s |
| Fixed + debug delay + KASAN | **PASS**: all 200 iterations completed,
no KASAN/warnings |

### Fix

The fix is in `select_collect()` — stop counting dead dentries and
`DCACHE_SHRINK_LIST` dentries as "found":

```diff
--- a/fs/dcache.c
+++ b/fs/dcache.c
@@ -1448,13 +1459,27 @@ static enum d_walk_ret select_collect(void
*_data, struct dentry *dentry)
 	if (data->start == dentry)
 		goto out;
 
-	if (dentry->d_flags & DCACHE_SHRINK_LIST) {
-		data->found++;
+	if (dentry->d_lockref.count < 0) {
+		/*
+		 * Dead dentry (lockref_mark_dead sets count
negative).
+		 * Another CPU is in the middle of __dentry_kill() and
+		 * will shortly unlink it from the tree.  Do not count
+		 * it as "found" --- that causes
shrink_dcache_parent()
+		 * to loop indefinitely.
+		 */
+	} else if (dentry->d_flags & DCACHE_SHRINK_LIST) {
+		/*
+		 * Already on a shrink list, being processed by
another
+		 * path (e.g., the global LRU shrinker).  Do not count
+		 * it as "found" --- if the other path is slow (e.g.,
+		 * contention on d_lock or filesystem callbacks),
+		 * shrink_dcache_parent() would spin forever waiting
for
+		 * them to finish.  The other shrinker will handle
these
+		 * dentries.
+		 */
 	} else if (!dentry->d_lockref.count) {
 		to_shrink_list(dentry, &data->dispose);
 		data->found++;
-	} else if (dentry->d_lockref.count < 0) {
-		data->found++;
 	}
```

**Why this is correct:**

- **Dead dentries (`count < 0`)**: These are being killed by another
CPU's `__dentry_kill()`. That CPU will call `dentry_unlist()` to remove
them from the parent's children list. `shrink_dcache_parent()` doesn't
need to wait for them — they'll disappear from the tree on their own.

- **`DCACHE_SHRINK_LIST` dentries**: These are already on another
shrinker's dispose list and will be processed by that path. Counting
them as "found" forces `shrink_dcache_parent()` to wait for the other
shrinker to finish, which can take arbitrarily long (especially with
filesystem callbacks or the debug delay).

- **The `select_collect2` path** (used when `data.found > 0` but
`data.dispose` is empty) handles `DCACHE_SHRINK_LIST` dentries
separately by setting `data->victim` and processing them directly. With
this fix, `select_collect2` is only reached when there are genuinely
unprocessable dentries (count > 0, not dead, not on shrink list), not
when there are merely in-flight kills or concurrent shrinkers.

### Relationship to the production UAF crash

The livelock is the **precursor** to the use-after-free crash seen in
production (P2260313060):

1. Without the debug delay, the `__dentry_kill` race window is
nanoseconds (just `cond_resched()`).
2. Most of the time, the dead dentry is unlinked before
`select_collect` finds it → no issue.
3. Occasionally, `select_collect` finds dead dentries and spins
briefly. During this spinning, the specific timing allows a parent
dentry to be fully freed (via `dentry_free` → `call_rcu` → slab
reclaim) and its slab page reused for `kmalloc-96`.
4. When the spinning `shrink_dcache_parent` or a concurrent
`__dentry_kill` then accesses the freed parent → UAF crash.

The fix prevents the spinning entirely, eliminating both the livelock
and the UAF.

review_comments.author: Al Viro
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Al Viro responded sarcastically to the patch, questioning the relevance of a Turing Test in this context and implying that the patch's purpose is unclear.

Reviewer Al Viro questioned the patch's description of resolving livelock through specific timing, pointing out that the parent dentry reference is not dropped until after the child is detached and DCACHE_DENTRY_KILLED is set on it, which makes it unclear how the described timing resolves the issue.

Reviewer Al Viro questioned the connection between the proposed fix for a livelock issue and the reported UAF bug, asking how the busy-waiting in the patch would lead to a use-after-free condition.

Reviewer Al Viro pointed out that the patch incorrectly assumes a dentry is being freed on its own shrink list, and instead should be handled by shrink_kill(), which would prevent the WARN_ON_ONCE() from triggering unnecessarily.

Reviewer Al Viro noted that the current patch does not handle a race condition where another thread's shrink list can contain dentries from a filesystem being shut down, and suggested considering calls from shrink_dcache_for_umount() to address this issue.

Reviewer Al Viro strongly disagreed with the patch's analysis of a potential UAF bug, stating that it lacks explanation and contains incorrect information about the path to the bug.
review_comments.raw_body:
Other than rather uncharitable ones about the usefulness of the Turing Test,
you mean?

---

Livelock is real and known, all right, but do explain what does "resolves
through specific timing that allows a parent dentry to be freed" mean.
Especially since the reference to parent is *not* dropped until after
having child detached from the tree and DCACHE_DENTRY_KILLED on it,
with ->d_lock on child held over that.  So select_collect2() seeing
the victim still locked and attached to the tree has to happen before
the grace period for parent has a chance to begin.  And rcu_read_lock()
grabbed there prevents that grace period from completing until we
do the matching rcu_read_unlock() in shrink_dcache_parent().

---

Yes.  And?  That's the livelock, all right, and it needs fixing,
but how does busy-wait here lead to UAF on anything?

---

They have no business going into data.dispose; for fuck sake, dentries
on somebody else's shrink list are explicitly fed to shrink_kill().

---

... and since they keep their parents busy, we should not leave until
they are gone.  For real fun, consider calls from shrink_dcache_for_umount() -
and yes, it *is* possible for another thread's shrink list to contain
dentries from filesystem being shut down.  Legitimately so.

---

... and it still offers zero explanation of the path from livelock to
UAF.  It may or may not be real, but there's nothing in all that verbiage
even suggesting what it might be.  And proposed analysis is flat-out
wrong.

As for the livelock, see viro/vfs.git #work.dcache-busy-wait (in -next
as of today).

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged Al's feedback about potential races on the d_lru list, but expressed doubt that it's just bad luck and thinks it's more likely due to concurrent access.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for taking a look, Al. We'll keep looking at this thing and see
if we can collect more info, and come up with a better theory of the
crash. I'll dig deeper into the vmcore tomorrow.

FWIW, I've been running a bpftrace script across a swath of machines
that emulates the WARN_ON_ONCE() in the original patch I proposed.
We've had a few crashes while it's running and haven't yet collected
any stack traces.

The script only runs about 80% of the time though, so it's possible
we're just getting unlucky, but I sort of doubt it. It seems more
likely that these dentries are racing onto the list after we've passed
the check.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Al Viro
review_comments.summary: Al Viro noted that the WARN_ON_ONCE() added by Jeff Layton may trigger false positives because a dentry can be on a shrink list even if it's not being freed, and suggested modifying the logic to handle this case.
review_comments.raw_body:
OK...  FWIW, on the trees up to 7.0-rc7:
	* dentry passed to select_collect2() must be still attached to
the tree (no DCACHE_DENTRY_KILLED on it)
	* caller holds ->d_lock on that dentry.
	* in case when its ->d_lockref.count is positive, it's busy (at least
at the moment) and there's nothing to be done; move on.
	* in case when its ->d_lockref.count is negative, it's getting killed
right now.  That's a busy-wait case - we ignore it on this pass, but remember
that we'll need to rescan.
	* in case when its ->d_lockref.count is 0 and it's not on a shrink list:
it's evictable, move it to data.dispose, no problem.
	* in case when its ->d_lockref.count is 0 and it *is* on a shrink list:
try to steal it.  We can't do that from the d_walk() callback itself - it's
a non-blocking environment, to start with, so no evictions are possible there.
What we can do is to have it returned to shrink_dcache_tree() - grab rcu_read_lock()
to make sure it won't get freed (it hasn't reached dentry_unlist(), let alone
dentry_free(), so the grace period for freeing hasn't started yet) and tell d_walk()
to stop and return to caller immediately.
	Note that we deliberately return without rcu_read_unlock() - that's what
protects the victim from getting freed under us.  Other thread might or might not
get around to starting the eviction of the victim, but whether it does that or not,
it won't get around to freeing it.
	In shrink_dcache_tree() we grab ->d_lock on the victim again (it had been
dropped by d_walk() when the callback returned).  Then we call lock_for_kill(),
which starts with checking that ->d_lockref.count is 0.  If it isn't, there's
nothing to be done to that sucker; it either went busy (in which case we should
ignore it and move on) or somebody (likely the owner of the shrink list it had
been on) got around to evicting it; in the latter case we need to wait until
the damn thing is killed.  *IF* refcount is still 0, we carefully acquire the
->i_lock on its inode (if any).  Note that we are still holding rcu_read_lock(),
so it can't get freed under us even if we have to drop and regain ->d_lock.
We do need to recheck that refcount is still zero if we do that, obviously.
	Failing lock_for_kill() is either due to dentry becoming busy (and thus
to be skipped) or due to dentry having been passed to __dentry_kill().  In
the latter case we busy-wait, same as we'd do if we saw negative ->d_lockref.count
in select_collect2().
	Successful lock_for_kill() is followed by evicting the sucker;
we do *not* remove it (or its ancestors, if it had been the only thing
holding them busy) from whatever shrink list they'd been on.  Anything on
shrink lists gets reduced to the state of passive chunk of memory no
longer connected to filesystem objects, marked with DCACHE_DENTRY_KILLED
and left for the owner of shrink list to free once it gets around to that.
There's no urgency anymore -
		rcu_read_lock();
		if (!lock_for_kill(dentry)) {
			bool can_free;
			rcu_read_unlock();
			d_shrink_del(dentry);
			can_free = dentry->d_flags & DCACHE_DENTRY_KILLED;
			spin_unlock(&dentry->d_lock);
			if (can_free)
				dentry_free(dentry);
			continue;
		}
on the shrink_dentry_list() side will have lock_for_kill() return false
(->d_lockref.count already negative), remove the sucker from the list,
see DCACHE_DENTRY_KILLED on it, unlock the sucker and free it.  Note that
nothing in that sequence touches any fs objects - it's just a disposal of
inert chunk of memory now.
	Note that we can't be stealing from ourselves - if anything had
been added to data.dispose, d_walk() sees D_WALK_QUIT or D_WALK_NORETRY
and either buggers off immediately or sets retry to false.  It won't
restart walking the tree in either case, so there's no way for the same
dentry to be revisited by it.

	Having one shrink_dentry_tree() steal from another is OK - see
the conditions when shrink_dentry_list() and __dentry_kill() are calling
dentry_free() (called 'can_free' in both).

	Getting rid of busy-wait is handled with fairly small modification
to that: select_collect2() treats negative refcount same as it would
treat 0-and-on-shrink-list case and shrink_dcache_tree() checks if the
victim has negative refcount and no DCACHE_DENTRY_KILLED yet.  In that
case it adds a local object (struct completion_list node) to a list hanging
off dentry->waiters, evicts whatever else it might've collected, then waits
for dentry_unlist() on the victim to have called complete() on the
node.completion for everything on ->waiters.
	The rest of the logics is unchanged - if victim is not in the
middle of eviction, we try to steal it, etc., same as in the mainline.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] dcache: warn when a dentry is freed with a non-empty ->d_lru
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch warns when a dentry (directory entry) is freed while it still has entries in its ->d_lru list, which can indicate a racing condition or other issue.

review_comments.author: Al Viro
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Al Viro responded sarcastically to the patch description, questioning its relevance and usefulness.

Reviewer Al Viro questioned how a parent dentry can be freed and reused before its reference is dropped, which would allow select_collect2() to see the victim still locked and attached to the tree, leading to a livelock.

Reviewer Al Viro noted that the livelock in shrink_dcache_parent() is a known issue, but questioned how it leads to use-after-free (UAF) crashes.

Reviewer Al Viro pointed out that the patch incorrectly frees dentries on another process's shrink list and suggested they should be handled by shrink_kill() instead.

Reviewer Al Viro noted that the patch does not address a potential livelock caused by concurrent dentry invalidation and global dcache shrinker, which can lead to use-after-free crashes. He suggested considering calls from shrink_dcache_for_umount() where another thread's shrink list may contain dentries from a filesystem being shut down.

Reviewer Al Viro criticized the patch's explanation of a use-after-free bug, stating that it lacks clear analysis and contains incorrect information about the path to the bug.
review_comments.raw_body:
Other than rather uncharitable ones about the usefulness of the Turing Test,
you mean?

---

Livelock is real and known, all right, but do explain what does "resolves
through specific timing that allows a parent dentry to be freed" mean.
Especially since the reference to parent is *not* dropped until after
having child detached from the tree and DCACHE_DENTRY_KILLED on it,
with ->d_lock on child held over that.  So select_collect2() seeing
the victim still locked and attached to the tree has to happen before
the grace period for parent has a chance to begin.  And rcu_read_lock()
grabbed there prevents that grace period from completing until we
do the matching rcu_read_unlock() in shrink_dcache_parent().

---

Yes.  And?  That's the livelock, all right, and it needs fixing,
but how does busy-wait here lead to UAF on anything?

---

They have no business going into data.dispose; for fuck sake, dentries
on somebody else's shrink list are explicitly fed to shrink_kill().

---

... and since they keep their parents busy, we should not leave until
they are gone.  For real fun, consider calls from shrink_dcache_for_umount() -
and yes, it *is* possible for another thread's shrink list to contain
dentries from filesystem being shut down.  Legitimately so.

---

... and it still offers zero explanation of the path from livelock to
UAF.  It may or may not be real, but there's nothing in all that verbiage
even suggesting what it might be.  And proposed analysis is flat-out
wrong.

As for the livelock, see viro/vfs.git #work.dcache-busy-wait (in -next
as of today).

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges a fix is needed to address a livelock in shrink_dcache_parent() caused by a race between concurrent proc dentry invalidation and the global dentry shrinker, which leads to use-after-free crashes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for taking a look, Al. We'll keep looking at this thing and see
if we can collect more info, and come up with a better theory of the
crash. I'll dig deeper into the vmcore tomorrow.

FWIW, I've been running a bpftrace script across a swath of machines
that emulates the WARN_ON_ONCE() in the original patch I proposed.
We've had a few crashes while it's running and haven't yet collected
any stack traces.

The script only runs about 80% of the time though, so it's possible
we're just getting unlucky, but I sort of doubt it. It seems more
likely that these dentries are racing onto the list after we've passed
the check.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Al Viro
review_comments.summary: Al Viro pointed out that select_collect2() must hold ->d_lock on the dentry and that lock_for_kill() is used to acquire ->i_lock on its inode if necessary, but noted that there's a busy-wait case when the refcount is negative and suggested modifying the logic to handle this case by adding a completion to the dentry->waiters list and waiting for it to be completed.
review_comments.raw_body:
OK...  FWIW, on the trees up to 7.0-rc7:
	* dentry passed to select_collect2() must be still attached to
the tree (no DCACHE_DENTRY_KILLED on it)
	* caller holds ->d_lock on that dentry.
	* in case when its ->d_lockref.count is positive, it's busy (at least
at the moment) and there's nothing to be done; move on.
	* in case when its ->d_lockref.count is negative, it's getting killed
right now.  That's a busy-wait case - we ignore it on this pass, but remember
that we'll need to rescan.
	* in case when its ->d_lockref.count is 0 and it's not on a shrink list:
it's evictable, move it to data.dispose, no problem.
	* in case when its ->d_lockref.count is 0 and it *is* on a shrink list:
try to steal it.  We can't do that from the d_walk() callback itself - it's
a non-blocking environment, to start with, so no evictions are possible there.
What we can do is to have it returned to shrink_dcache_tree() - grab rcu_read_lock()
to make sure it won't get freed (it hasn't reached dentry_unlist(), let alone
dentry_free(), so the grace period for freeing hasn't started yet) and tell d_walk()
to stop and return to caller immediately.
	Note that we deliberately return without rcu_read_unlock() - that's what
protects the victim from getting freed under us.  Other thread might or might not
get around to starting the eviction of the victim, but whether it does that or not,
it won't get around to freeing it.
	In shrink_dcache_tree() we grab ->d_lock on the victim again (it had been
dropped by d_walk() when the callback returned).  Then we call lock_for_kill(),
which starts with checking that ->d_lockref.count is 0.  If it isn't, there's
nothing to be done to that sucker; it either went busy (in which case we should
ignore it and move on) or somebody (likely the owner of the shrink list it had
been on) got around to evicting it; in the latter case we need to wait until
the damn thing is killed.  *IF* refcount is still 0, we carefully acquire the
->i_lock on its inode (if any).  Note that we are still holding rcu_read_lock(),
so it can't get freed under us even if we have to drop and regain ->d_lock.
We do need to recheck that refcount is still zero if we do that, obviously.
	Failing lock_for_kill() is either due to dentry becoming busy (and thus
to be skipped) or due to dentry having been passed to __dentry_kill().  In
the latter case we busy-wait, same as we'd do if we saw negative ->d_lockref.count
in select_collect2().
	Successful lock_for_kill() is followed by evicting the sucker;
we do *not* remove it (or its ancestors, if it had been the only thing
holding them busy) from whatever shrink list they'd been on.  Anything on
shrink lists gets reduced to the state of passive chunk of memory no
longer connected to filesystem objects, marked with DCACHE_DENTRY_KILLED
and left for the owner of shrink list to free once it gets around to that.
There's no urgency anymore -
		rcu_read_lock();
		if (!lock_for_kill(dentry)) {
			bool can_free;
			rcu_read_unlock();
			d_shrink_del(dentry);
			can_free = dentry->d_flags & DCACHE_DENTRY_KILLED;
			spin_unlock(&dentry->d_lock);
			if (can_free)
				dentry_free(dentry);
			continue;
		}
on the shrink_dentry_list() side will have lock_for_kill() return false
(->d_lockref.count already negative), remove the sucker from the list,
see DCACHE_DENTRY_KILLED on it, unlock the sucker and free it.  Note that
nothing in that sequence touches any fs objects - it's just a disposal of
inert chunk of memory now.
	Note that we can't be stealing from ourselves - if anything had
been added to data.dispose, d_walk() sees D_WALK_QUIT or D_WALK_NORETRY
and either buggers off immediately or sets retry to false.  It won't
restart walking the tree in either case, so there's no way for the same
dentry to be revisited by it.

	Having one shrink_dentry_tree() steal from another is OK - see
the conditions when shrink_dentry_list() and __dentry_kill() are calling
dentry_free() (called 'can_free' in both).

	Getting rid of busy-wait is handled with fairly small modification
to that: select_collect2() treats negative refcount same as it would
treat 0-and-on-shrink-list case and shrink_dcache_tree() checks if the
victim has negative refcount and no DCACHE_DENTRY_KILLED yet.  In that
case it adds a local object (struct completion_list node) to a list hanging
off dentry->waiters, evicts whatever else it might've collected, then waits
for dentry_unlist() on the victim to have called complete() on the
node.completion for everything on ->waiters.
	The rest of the logics is unchanged - if victim is not in the
middle of eviction, we try to steal it, etc., same as in the mainline.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] fuse: fix io-uring background queue dispatch on request completion

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] fuse: fix io-uring background queue dispatch on request completion

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the io-uring path of the FUSE file system where background requests are not properly dispatched when they complete. The issue arises because the connection-level background counters (num_background and active_background) are not updated before flushing the queue's background queue, which can lead to reduced effective queue depth. To fix this, the patch updates the connection-level counters first before flushing the queue's background queue in the fuse_request_end function. This ensures that pending io-uring background requests on the queue are dispatched correctly.
